coredata.dc:title: In situ one-pot synthesis of chitosan@ P,N-CPDs as a ratiometric scatter up-conversion sensor for α-lipoic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdella, Aya A.
Hammad, Sherin F.
coredata.dc:description: The rapid sensitive determination of α-lipoic acid (ALA) is essential for monitoring biological functions. Nevertheless, the lack of chromophore and its occurrence in complex biological matrices renders its determination challenging and requires additional treatment steps which lengthen assay procedure and affects method sensitivity. Herein, through the coordination of frequency doubling scatter (FDS) of polysaccharide strands with up-conversion of the embedded in situ formed P, N- carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), a novel ratiometric sensing platform was constructed. One-pot synthesis of chitosan decorated P, N-CPDs (chitosan@ P,N-CPDs) was attempted through microwave-assisted crosslinking-accelerated carbonization, using phosphoric acid as crosslinker and dopant. The constructed platform responded to the presence of α-lipoic acid (ALA) in concentration dependent manner (R2 = 0.995) between 1.65–100 μg/mL, with detection limit of 0.55 μg/mL, good accuracy (%E = 0.52 %) and precision (%RSD < 2 %). Consistent results with comparison method confirmed sensor reliability for determination of ALA in urine, Thiotacid® and Thiotacid compound® tablets, with no interference from the coexisting highly absorbing water-soluble vitamins, thiamine, and cyanocobalamin, or other potential interferents. Thus, the developed high performance sensing platform could be adopted for ALA determination as it is much less prone to spectral interferences and hence more discerning than those operated in single wavelength mode.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Up-conversion
Carbonized polymer dots
Thioctic acid
α-lipoic acid
Frequency doubling scatter
Ratiometric sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for determination of ALA using P, N-CPDs
Assay of ALA in tablet dosage forms
Determination of ALA in real human urine samples
Characterization and optical properties of P, N-CPDs
Frequency doubling scatter and upconverted PL of P, N-CPDs
Determination of ALA using the prepared P, N-CPDs
Assay of ALA in pharmaceutical formulations using P, N-CPDs
Assay of ALA in urine using P, N-CPDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagrammatic representation of the developed sensor preparation and measured response for ALA detection.

(A) Overlay of UV/Vis, excitation, and emission spectra of P, N-CPDs, inset photos of P, N-CPDs solution under daylight (1) and UV light 365 nm (2). (B) FTIR spectrum of chitosan and P, N-CPDs showing changes in surface functional groups.

(A) TEM images of P, N-CPDs showing spherical uniform particle size (inset particle size distribution), (B) HRTEM images of the P, N-CPDs showing lattice fringe of 0.20 nm (inset lattice fringe measurement), (D) SAED pattern of P, N-CPDs demonstrating its crystallinity, (D) TEM image of the proposed P, N-CPDs after addition of ALA showing aggregated particles.

(A) Overlay of upconverted PL spectra of P, N-CPDs and P, N-CPDs/ALA (100 μg/mL) recorded at Ex 710 nm. (B) Stern-Volmer plot for P, N-CPDs/ALA at different temperatures, (C) Lineweaver-Burk plot for P, N-CPDs/ALA at different temperatures.

(A) Selectivity of the proposed FDS/UCE ratiometric probe for ALA (250.0 μM) over other possible matrix interfering substances (thiol compounds: 250.0 μM, metals: 10.0 μM, others: 50.0 μM). (Asterisk (*) indicates significant change attained by ALA, ANOVA, p = 1.12 x 10−34). (B) anti interference experiment showing sensor’s reliability for ALA analysis in complex matrices.
Assay result for determination of ALA in pharmaceutical formulation using the proposed P, N-CPDs ratiometric sensor.
Application of the proposed P, N-CPDs ratiometric sensing probe for ALA determination in urine.

head.ce:title: In situ one-pot synthesis of chitosan@ P,N-CPDs as a ratiometric scatter up-conversion sensor for α-lipoic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Elgeish Street, the medical campus of Tanta University, Tanta, 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: the medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid sensitive determination of α-lipoic acid (ALA) is essential for monitoring biological functions. Nevertheless, the lack of chromophore and its occurrence in complex biological matrices renders its determination challenging and requires additional treatment steps which lengthen assay procedure and affects method sensitivity. Herein, through the coordination of frequency doubling scatter (FDS) of polysaccharide strands with up-conversion of the embedded in situ formed P, N- carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), a novel ratiometric sensing platform was constructed. One-pot synthesis of chitosan decorated P, N-CPDs (chitosan@ P,N-CPDs) was attempted through microwave-assisted crosslinking-accelerated carbonization, using phosphoric acid as crosslinker and dopant. The constructed platform responded to the presence of α-lipoic acid (ALA) in concentration dependent manner (R2 = 0.995) between 1.65–100 μg/mL, with detection limit of 0.55 μg/mL, good accuracy (%E = 0.52 %) and precision (%RSD < 2 %). Consistent results with comparison method confirmed sensor reliability for determination of ALA in urine, Thiotacid® and Thiotacid compound® tablets, with no interference from the coexisting highly absorbing water-soluble vitamins, thiamine, and cyanocobalamin, or other potential interferents. Thus, the developed high performance sensing platform could be adopted for ALA determination as it is much less prone to spectral interferences and hence more discerning than those operated in single wavelength mode.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: α-lipoic acid (ALA) or thioctic acid, (R)-5-(1,2-Dithiolan-3-yl) pentanoic acid, is an organosulfur compound derived from octanoic acid (Fig. 1 ) [1]. It is a well-known endogenous antioxidant that is naturally produced in human body and also found in healthy food and dietary supplements beside its use in pharmaceuticals for patients with elevated blood ketoacids [2]. As reported in literature, 10 % of administered ALA is excreted in urine with reported concentration ranging from 10 – 700 μg/mL [3]. Recently, ALA level in body has been linked to insulin autoimmune syndrome which necessitates its sensitive determination in biological matrices [4,5]. Nevertheless, the sensitive direct determination of ALA is challenging as it lacks chromophore and usually occurs in complex matrices with many coexisting strong light absorbers.

ALA determination is attempted spectrophotometrically after oxidation [6], or using capillary electrophoresis [7], in addition to some chromatographic methods using various detection modes, including either direct HPLC-UV [8], HPLC-MS [9,10], HPLC-coulometric detection [11], or indirect derivatization-based using fluorogenic [12–14], chromogenic [15], or chemiluminescence reagents [16]. Also, ALA is determined using different electrochemical methods [17–19] as well as Cu2+ modulated cadmium [20] and carbon quantum dot sensors [21]. Surprisingly, the application of ratiometric scatter up-conversion sensor would enable single step fast sensitive accurate and precise determination of ALA, even in the presence of highly absorbing co existing compounds, without any separation steps. This would eventually enhance both throughput and greenness of the analytical procedures.

Ratiometric sensors have been developed to address the limitations encountered with single response mode sensing probes, which may lead to inaccurate readings due to variations in probe concentration solvent composition, chemical interferences, and background autofluorescence [22]. Utilizing ratiometric sensors entails recording the intensities of two or more peaks to monitor changes in a specific probe in response to a particular analyte, thus embodying the principle of internal standard [23]. Commonly, this ratiometric strategy reduces intrinsic errors and exhibits a significant ability to further improve the accuracy and precision of the measurements [24]. The construction of such ratiometric sensors requires special dual emitting materials that should fulfill three criteria, including no overlap between the two emissions, both peaks are generated by single excitation, and respond independently to target analyte, which are seldom and hard to obtain [23].

Recently, the emergence of fluorescence-scatter ratiometric sensors could replace dual emitters by utilizing first order scattering (FOS), second order scattering (SOS), or frequency doubling scattering (FDS) which are located at the excitation wavelength, double the excitation wavelength, and half the excitation wavelength, respectively [25 26]. Regarding FDS band, in which two photons of the same frequency combine to generate a new photon with twice the energy of the initial photon upon interaction with certain material, is mainly affected by both surface properties, particle shape and size, in addition to scatterer concentration [27]. Thus, it can be utilized for sensing purposes to defeat chemical interferences.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) belong to a recently emerging subclass of carbon dots (CDs) characterized by a carbonized core and an organic polymer chain coat [28]. CPDs are usually prepared from polymers, such as chitosan (CTS), cellulose, and polyvinylpyrrolidone, by the process of crosslinking-accelerated carbonization [29]. CPDs combine the advantages of both CDs and polymers with additional higher stability than polymers. In addition, the strategic adjustment of their photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) and surface binding sites can be attempted through doping and co-doping with heteroatoms [30,31]. Surprisingly, phosphorus doped CDs have been reported to exhibit photon up-conversion, a process exhibited by upconverting nanoparticles by which light is emitted at shorter wavelengths than those used for excitation [32,33], allowing their use in solar energy harvesting [34–36]. The incorporation of phosphorus in such a carbonized polymer matrix would afford a versatile sensing material utilizing both polymer scatter band and phosphorus doped CPDs’ up-conversion band. Also, spectral interferences from coexisting absorbers could be evaded by using photon up-conversion [37–39].

In this work, the coordination of frequency doubling scatter (FDS) of polysaccharide strands with up-conversion of the embedded in situ formed P, and N doped carbonized polymer dots (P, N- CPDs) was utilized to construct a novel ratiometric sensing platform for ALA. This was accomplished through a one-pot in situ synthesis of P, N-CPDs using CTS and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) in a domestic microwave in only 60 sec. In presence of ALA, CPDs were aggregated inducing quenching of up-conversion peak and enhancement of scatter peak achieving minimum background, and matrix interferences. The developed high performance sensing platform could be adopted for ALA determination as it is much less prone to spectral interferences and hence more discerning than those operated in single wavelength mode. It is worth noting that the developed sensor is cost effective, ecofriendly, and sustainable, as it adopted nontoxic precursors and consumed minimal amount of energy.

CTS (medium molecular weight, deacetylation degree 75–85 %) and H3PO4 (85 %, HPLC grade) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). ALA (99.98 %) was kindly supplied by Sigma for Pharmaceutical Industries (Quesna, Egypt). Cystine, cysteine, methionine, and glutathione were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Alpha Chemika (Mumbai, India). Potassium dihydrogen phosphate and anhydrous sodium acetate (analytical reagent grade) were purchased from Fischer Scientific (Loughborough, UK). Glacial acetic acid (HPLC grade) was purchased from Advent Chembio (Maharashtra, India). Thiamine (99.98 %), cyanocobolamine (99.98 %), tyrosine (99.98 %), and phenylalanine (99.8 %) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Mg stearate, NaCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, FeCL3, uric acid, and creatinine were obtained from Isochem (Vert-le-Petit, France). Thiotacid® (labelled to contain 600 mg ALA), and Thiotacid® compound (labelled to contain 600 mg ALA, 80 mg Vit B1, and 0.5 mg Vit B12), products of EVA Pharma for Pharmaceuticals and Medical Appliance (Alexandria, Egypt), were obtained from community pharmacy (Tanta, Egypt). Blank human urine samples were obtained from a healthy volunteer not taking medication for one week, filtered, centrifuged (6000 rpm for 5 min), and stored at 4˚C.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on FP-6300 spectrofluorometer (Jasco, Japan) operated with Spectra manager software (version 1.55). The scan speed and slit width were adjusted at 1000 nm/min and at 5 nm (for both excitation and emission), respectively. The UV spectra were recorded between 200–800 nm on V-530 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Jasco, Japan). FTIR-4100 (Jasco, Japan) was used for identification of surface functional groups of the obtained P, N-CPDs. Zeta potential measurements were conducted using MAL 7.02 Zetasizer (Malvern, UK) in a disposable zeta cell. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images, selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were conducted using JEM- 2100F High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan) operated at accelerated voltage of 200 mV. A domestic microwave oven (1000 W) (Tornado, Egypt), ultrasonic cleaner bath 3510 (Bransonic, USA), water bath (Memmert, Germany), pH211 pH-meter with double junction glass membrane electrode (HANNA, USA), and EBA20S centrifuge (Hettich, Germany) were utilized. Dialysis membrane MWCO 12 KDa (FREY Scientific, US) was used.

CTS solution (1 %) was prepared by dissolving 0.6 g in 1 % acetic acid with continuous stirring on magnetic stirrer for 4 h at room temperature. Then, 3 mL of H3PO4 (85 %) were added to CTS solution. The mixture was directly placed in domestic microwave and heated in open vessel (a 250-mL beaker) at microwave power of 100 % for 60 s until a dark brown residue is formed. Then, the formed residue was resuspended in 10 mL distilled water and placed in ultrasonic bath for 30 min. Afterwards, the solution was centrifuged for 10 min, filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filter, and dialyzed against distilled water for 1 h. Finally, P, N-CPDs solution (0.0015 μg/mL) was collected in dry clean falcon tube and stored at 4˚C until further utilized (Fig. 1).

The quantum yield (QY) of P, N-CPDs was measured relative to quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4) with standard QY of 54 % at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The relative QY (Qx) of P, N-CPDs was calculated using Eq. (1) [40,41].
(1)
Q X
=
Q
st
×
F x
F
st
×
η x
η
st
2
×
A
st
A x
where F refers to the fluorescence, A is the absorbance, x refers to P, N-CPDs, st refers to quinine sulfate and η refers to the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for determination of ALA using P, N-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: In separate 5-mL volumetric flasks, 150 μL of the prepared P, N-CPDs solution were mixed with different volumes (50–500 μL) of 1 mg/mL ALA dissolved in methanol. The solution was made up to total volume using distilled water. The emission spectra were recorded between 200 – 750 nm after excitation at 710 nm. The intensity of up-converted emission (UCE) and FDS peaks were recorded at 434 nm and 352 nm, respectively. FDS352/ UCE434 ratio were plotted against the corresponding ALA concentration to construct calibration curve and estimate the regression parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of ALA in tablet dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 20 tablets were finely powdered in a porcelain mortar. Then, an amount equivalent to one tablet was dissolved in 30 mL of methanol, and quantitively transferred into 50-mL volumetric flask where final volume was made up using the same solvent. The solution was then filtered, and the first 10-mL were discarded [42]. An aliquot of 830 μL of filtrate was diluted in 10-mL volumetric flask using distilled water. Different aliquots (50, 200, and 400 μL) were analyzed as previously described in section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALA in real human urine samples
ce:section.ce:para: Urine samples (490 μL) were spiked by adding 10 μL of ALA spiking solutions (500 – 5000 μg/mL) to obtain ALA concentration in urine between 10–120 μg/mL. Then, the samples were subjected to protein precipitation using 1 mL methanol to extract ALA, modified after Saito et al [43]. The samples were then vortexed for 1 min and centrifuged (6000 rpm for 10 min). The resulting supernatant was transferred into a 5-mL volumetric flask. Then, P, N-CPDs solution (150 μL) was added, and the solution was made up to total volume using distilled water. Upconverted emission spectra were recorded after excitation at 710 nm, and calibration plots were constructed as previously mentioned in section 2.5.

CTS is a natural poly glucosamine that has been reported to be crosslinked by diprotic acids through electrostatic interaction [44]. Various researchers have reported its utilization as a carbon source for preparation of CPDs through heating, either in an oven or a microwave, certain period ranging from 15 min (microwave) to 24 h (hydrothermal autoclave) [45]. In this work, H3PO4 was chosen as a crosslinker and dopant due to its affinity to crosslink CTS and its safety compared to other crosslinkers. Both microwave time and H3PO4volume were studied and the % QY of the resulting P, N-CPDs was estimated. As shown in Fig S1A, maximum % QY was attempted using 3 mL of H3PO4. The effect of microwave time was investigated between 15 and 90 sec, to determine the shortest time required for achieving maximum QY. As presented in Fig. S1B, microwave heating for 60 sec was found optimum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of P, N-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared P, N-CPDs were characterized by investigating their optical properties and inspecting their morphological characteristics. The obtained P, N-CPDs solution exhibited intense blue PL under UV light (365 nm), as shown in Fig. 2 A. Maximum PL emission was observed at 408 nm after excitation at 320 nm (Fig. 2A). Moreover, the demonstrated excitation dependent emission (Fig. S2) indicated CDs formation [46]. Furthermore, the obtained P, N-CPDs showed excitation dependent emission indicating carbonization. The UV spectra of the obtained materials showed a peak at 252, and 320 which correspond to п-п* (CC and CN), and n- п* (O, N, and P), respectively [40]. In addition, an extended absorption peak after 365 nm was observed which can be ascribed to a surface functional moieties such as NH2 [47]. The P, N-CPDs showed pH dependent PL which significantly decreased (ANOVA p > 0.05) as pH was raised above 7.0 (Fig. S3) indicating that the formed P, N-CPDs were functionalized with CTS residues (pka = 6.5) [48].

The surface properties of the prepared P, N-CPDs were investigated using FTIR spectroscopy and zeta potential. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2B) showed medium intensity bands between 800–1100 cm−1 ascribed to C-O-P and PO stretches, strong forked band between 3300–3500 cm−1 corresponding to NH2 stretch, in addition to CN and CC stretches at 1630 cm−1 [49], while that of CTS showed OH, and residual CO at 3500 and 1600 cm−1 [45]. Additionally, the zeta potential measurement showed + 17.8 mV indicating positively charged surface functionality leading to electrostatic repulsion, imparting stability [50], and increasing ALA binding affinity [51].

The elemental composition of the synthesized CPDs was determined using EDX-TEM and showed high doping efficiency as indicated by high P (13.43 %) and N (20.21 %), and polymeric carbon dots indicated by high O (26.41 %) (Fig. S4). Moreover, HRTEM was employed to visualize the prepared P, N-CPDs (Fig. 3 ). HRTEM images showed spherical polymer particles within 2.5–8.0 nm with an average particle size of 4.7 ± 1.0 nm (Fig. 3A) in which small CPDs were embedded with lattice fringe of 0.20 nm (Fig. 3B). The crystallinity was further confirmed using SAED pattern (Fig. 3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Frequency doubling scatter and upconverted PL of P, N-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared P, N-CPDs showed excitation dependent upconverted PL at 434 nm after excitation at 710 nm, as shown in Fig. S5. This property has been reported in literature for phosphorus doped CPDs to enable their application as energy harvesting material. This upconverted emission peak showed clear dependence on the amount of H3PO4, as shown in Fig. S6, where CTS was treated under the same preparation conditions without H3PO4 and in the presence of different H3PO4 amounts. In the absence of H3PO4, a strong FDS peak was observed at 352 nm with no upconverted emission (Fig. S6A). Upon addition of H3PO4, the intensity of FDS peak started to decrease due to carbonization and decreased polymer character (Fig. S6B). When a larger H3PO4 volume was added during carbonization, a marked decrease in the FDS and increase in UCE were observed (Fig. S6 C and D). On the other hand, excessive H3PO4 addition resulted in complete disappearance of both FDS and UCE (Fig. S6E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALA using the prepared P, N-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of ALA resulted in marked quenching of the upconverted emision at 434 nm along with significant enhancement of FDS peak at 352 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 . As reported in literature, the intesity of scatter peaks is mainly dependent on particle size of the scatterer (CTS).The enhancement of FDS peak was assumed to be enhanced through P, N-CPDs aggregation induced mechanism. To investigate the underlying processes, TEM images were acquired for P, N-CPDs/ALA revealing evident aggregation, shown in Fig. 3D, which suggested possible static quenching of UCE [31]. Therefore, the kinetics of the quenching process was investigated using Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (2)) revealing static quenching as a possible mechanism where quenching efficiency was substanially reduced at higher temperatures [40] (Fig. 4 B).

Moreover, the binding constant was calculated for the formed constant at different temperatures using Lineweaver-Burk equation (Eq. (3)) [52]. The corresponding KSV and K values, shown in Fig. 4 C and Table S1, indicated that the stability of the formd complex decreased considerably as the temprature was raised.
(2)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
(3)
1
( I
0
- I )
=
1
I 0
+
1
k
I 0
1
[ Q ]
Where I0
and I are the PPL intensities of P, N-CPDs in the absence and presence of the quencher (ALA), respectively, [Q] is the quencher concentration, and KSV and k are the Stern-Volmer constant and binding constant, respectively.

For further understanding of P, N-CPDs and ALA interaction, the effect pf pH on the quenching effiency was further investigated (Fig. S7). The potential contribution of ionic interaction between ALA carboxylic group (pka = 4.7) with primary amine (NH2) residues of CTS (pka = 6.5–7.0) was evident as indicated by maximum quenching effeicency achieved at pH between 5.0–7.0, at which both groups are maintained in ionized form [51]. Thus, this ALA induced aggregation could markedly enhance the FDS peak while that of UCE was quenched.

A ratiometric sensing platform was constructed for ALA determination using P, N-CPDs based on recording the ratio of FDS352/ UCE434. The recorded FDS352/ UCE434 was dependent on ALA concentration in the solution (Fig. S8A). The developed sensing strategy showed very good linearity (Fig. S8B) between 10.0 – 100.0 μg/mL (48.47–484.68 μM) as indicated by coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.995 along with other regression parameters (Table S2). Limit of detection (LOD) was calculated (3.3 σ/S: σ is the SD of the intercept and S is the slope of calibration curve) to be 0.55 μg/mL (Table S2). The accuracy of the developed sensing strategy was evaluated using three different ALA concentrations, three determinations for each. Excellent accuracy was indicated by % recovery values between 98–100 % and %E of 0.52 % (Table S3). Moreover, acceptable precision was indicated by %RSD < 2 % at all concentration levels (Table S4). Furthermore, the measured response was stable for 30 min which ensures method reliability (Fig. S9).

Moreover, potential interfering material such as, biothiols (250 μM), including cysteine, cystine, glutathione and methionine, co-formulated drug substances/ excipients (thiamine, cyanocobalamin, and Mg stearate), and other matrix components (e.g., Na, Mg, Ca, Fe, uric acid, creatinine, tyrosine, and phenylalanine) were examined to evaluate P, N-CPDs sensor reliability. As shown in Fig. 5 A, only ALA could achieve considerable response (ANOVA, p = 1.12 x10−34). In addition, to investigate sensor’s feasibility in analysis of ALA in complex samples, an anti-interference experiment was performed (Fig. 5 B) showing no significant change in the recorded response in presence of potential matrix interferents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of ALA in pharmaceutical formulations using P, N-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the applicability and reliability of the P, N-CPDs ratiometric sensing platform, it was applied for ALA in Thiotacid® and Thiotacid compound® tablets. Recovery results are presented in Table 1 which shows the ability of the developed senor to detect and quantify ALA in the presence of co-formulated drug substances and excipients. The obtained results were statistically compared with a reported comparison method. The reported procedure is based on oxidation of ALA using potassium permanganate in strong alkaline medium and recording the intensity of the developed color of the resulting manganate ions at 610 nm [53]. Both F-test and t-test showed consistent result as indicated by p-value > 0.05 for both dosage forms. It is worthy to note that the proposed ratiometric sensor demonstrated comparable sensitivity to reported chemical reaction methods with superior selectivity, speed, and greenness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of ALA in urine using P, N-CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: ALA determination in biological spiked samples (e.g., urine) was performed to further investigate the reliability of the developed sensing probe in a more complex matrix. Calibration curve was found linear over the range (10–120 μg/mL) with the regression equation: FDS352/UCE434 = 0.0095 CALA + 0.051 (R2 = 0.991). Results presented in Table 2 demonstrated the reliability of the FDS/UCE ratiometric sensor for practical application.

In this work, a novel P, N-CPDs-based ratiometric sensing probe was introduced for ALA determination utilizing upconverted emission and frequency doubling scatter signals. P, N-CPDs were prepared using facile and rapid single step one-pot method utilizing microwave pyrolysis (for 60 sec) employing CTS and H3PO4 as precursors. The prepared P, N-CPDs exhibited upconverted emission peak at 434 nm as well as a weak FDS at 352 nm. Upon the addition of ALA, UCE was quenched while FDS was enhanced. The underlying mechanism was attributed to P, N-CPDs aggregation resulting in static quenching and increased particle size. The presented ratiometric sensing platform exhibited good sensitivity, selectivity, simplicity, fast response, and green performance. Additionally, this work paves the way for the utilization of single emitting CPDs in ratiomtric sensing.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Aya A. Abdella: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – original draft. Sherin F. Hammad: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111123.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Introducing the Latest Self-healing Polymer Based on Thioctic Acid into the Undergraduate Chemistry Laboratory
J. Chem. Educ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Luo, C. Shi, Z. Wang, M. Chen, D.-H. Qu, Introducing the Latest Self-healing Polymer Based on Thioctic Acid into the Undergraduate Chemistry Laboratory, J. Chem. Educ. 99 (2022) 3488–3496. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jchemed.2c00463.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of free α-lipoic acid in foodstuffs by HPLC coupled with CEAD and ESI-MS
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.I. Durrani, H. Schwartz, M. Nagl, G. Sontag, Determination of free α-lipoic acid in foodstuffs by HPLC coupled with CEAD and ESI-MS, Food Chem. 120 (2010) 1143–1148. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2009.11.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Observations on the Urinary Excretion of Thioctic Acid
Am. J. Clin. Nutr.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.L. PATTERSON, H.P. BROQUIST, M.H. VON SALTZA, A. ALBRECHT, E.L.R. STOKSTAD, T.H. JUKES, Observations on the Urinary Excretion of Thioctic Acid, Am. J. Clin. Nutr. 4 (1956) 269–274. https://doi.org/10.1093/ajcn/4.3.269.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lipoamide or lipoic acid stimulates mitochondrial biogenesis in 3T3-L1 adipocytes via the endothelial NO synthase-cGMP-protein kinase G signalling pathway
Br. J. Pharmacol.
